metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.board: Maharashtra State Bureau of Textbook Production and Curriculum Research, Pune

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter introduces the fundamental building blocks of geometry including points, lines, and planes, and explores the concepts of coordinates, distance, betweenness, and the structure of mathematical proofs.
learning_objectives: Understand the undefined terms: point, line, and plane.
Calculate the distance between two points using coordinates on a number line.
Identify the concept of betweenness among collinear points.
Distinguish between conditional statements and their converses.
Understand the difference between postulates and theorems.
Learn the methods of direct and indirect proofs.

content_chunks.heading: Historical Context of Geometry
content_chunks.explanation: Geometry is a subject that evolved from the practical need to measure land. The word itself comes from the Greek words 'Geo' (Earth) and 'Metria' (measuring). Ancient civilizations like Egypt used geometry to build massive structures like the pyramids. Greek mathematicians like Thales contributed significantly; Thales is credited with determining the height of a pyramid using its shadow and similar triangles. Ancient Indians also possessed deep geometrical knowledge, as evidenced by the 'shulba-sutras' which described how to build altars using measurements. Renowned Indian mathematicians like Aaryabhat, Varahamihir, Bramhagupta, and Bhaskaracharya have made valuable contributions to the field.
key_points: Geometry originated from the need to measure land.
Thales used similar triangles to measure pyramid heights.
Vedic Indians used geometry for altar construction as described in shulba-sutras.
examples: Pyramids in Egypt built 3000 years before the Christian Era.

content_chunks.heading: Point, Line and Plane
content_chunks.explanation: In geometry, numbers, points, lines, and planes are considered basic concepts that are not formally defined. Lines and planes are essentially sets of points. In this context, the word 'line' always refers to a 'straight line'.
key_points: Points, lines, and planes are undefined terms.
Lines and planes are sets of points.
A line is understood to be a straight line.

common_doubts.q: Why are points and lines not defined?
common_doubts.a: They are considered fundamental entities from which other definitions are built; defining them would require even more basic terms, leading to an infinite regress.

content_chunks.heading: Co-ordinates of points and distance
content_chunks.explanation: On a number line, every point is associated with a number called its coordinate. To find the distance between any two points, we subtract the smaller coordinate from the larger coordinate. Distance is always a non-negative real number. If two points are distinct, the distance is positive; if they are the same point, the distance is zero. The distance between points A and B is denoted as d(A, B), which is also the length of segment AB, denoted as l(AB).
key_points: Distance = Larger Coordinate - Smaller Coordinate.
Distance is always a positive number or zero.
d(A, B) is the same as l(AB).

formulas.name: Distance Formula on Number Line
formulas.latex: d(P, Q) = x_2 - x_1 \text{ where } x_2 > x_1
formulas.description: Subtract the smaller coordinate from the larger coordinate to find the distance.

examples: If coordinate of A is -5 and B is -3, then d(A, B) = -3 - (-5) = 2.
If coordinate of D is 1 and E is 3, then d(D, E) = 3 - 1 = 2.

common_doubts.a: No, distance is a measure of magnitude and is always non-negative.

content_chunks.heading: Concept of Betweenness
content_chunks.explanation: If P, Q, and R are three distinct collinear points, point Q is said to be between P and R if and only if the distance from P to Q plus the distance from Q to R equals the distance from P to R. This is written as P - Q - R.
key_points: Points must be collinear for betweenness to apply.
P - Q - R implies d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) = d(P, R).

formulas.name: Betweenness Condition
formulas.latex: d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) = d(P, R)
formulas.description: Mathematical condition for point Q to be between P and R.

examples: If d(A, B) = 5, d(B, C) = 11, and d(A, C) = 6, then 5 + 6 = 11, so A is between B and C (B - A - C).

content_chunks.heading: Conditional statements and converse
content_chunks.explanation: A conditional statement is a statement written in the 'If-then' form. The part following 'If' is the antecedent (given part), and the part following 'then' is the consequent (part to be proved). If the antecedent and consequent are interchanged, the resulting statement is called the converse. A true conditional statement does not guarantee that its converse is also true.
key_points: Conditional form: If [antecedent], then [consequent].
Converse: If [consequent], then [antecedent].
A statement and its converse are not always both true.
examples: Statement: If a quadrilateral is a rhombus, then its diagonals are perpendicular bisectors. Converse: If the diagonals of a quadrilateral are perpendicular bisectors, then it is a rhombus. (Both are true).
Statement: If a number is a prime number, then it is even or odd. Converse: If a number is even or odd, then it is a prime number. (Statement is true, Converse is false).

content_chunks.heading: Euclidean Geometry and Proofs
content_chunks.explanation: Euclid, a Greek mathematician, organized geometry into a logical system. He defined 'Postulates' as self-evident geometrical statements accepted without proof. Statements that are proved logically using postulates and previously proved properties are called 'Theorems'. The logical argument to prove a theorem is called its 'Proof'. Proofs can be 'Direct' (starting from given facts to reach the conclusion) or 'Indirect' (assuming the conclusion is false and showing this leads to a contradiction).
key_points: Postulates are accepted without proof.
Theorems must be proved logically.
Direct proof uses deductive reasoning.
Indirect proof uses proof by contradiction.
examples: Euclid's Postulate: There is one and only one line passing through two points.
Euclid's Postulate: A circle of given radius can be drawn taking any point as its centre.

content_chunks.heading: Theorem: Vertically Opposite Angles
content_chunks.explanation: This theorem states that the opposite angles formed by two intersecting lines are of equal measures.
key_points: Given: Line AB and CD intersect at O.
To Prove: \angle AOC = \angle BOD.
Proof uses the property of angles in a linear pair.

formulas.name: Linear Pair Property
formulas.description: Angles in a linear pair are supplementary.

examples: Proof: \angle AOC + \angle BOC = 180^\circ (Linear pair). \angle BOC + \angle BOD = 180^\circ (Linear pair). Therefore, \angle AOC + \angle BOC = \angle BOC + \angle BOD, which simplifies to \angle AOC = \angle BOD.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter explores the properties of angles formed by parallel lines and a transversal, establishes theorems for interior, corresponding, and alternate angles, and provides tests to determine if two lines are parallel.
learning_objectives: Define parallel lines as coplanar lines that do not intersect.
Identify pairs of corresponding, alternate, and interior angles formed by a transversal.
Prove and apply the Interior Angle Theorem.
Prove and apply the Corresponding Angles and Alternate Angles Theorems.
Understand and use the sum of angles of a triangle property.
Apply tests for parallelness (Interior, Alternate, and Corresponding angles tests).

content_chunks.heading: Parallel Lines and Transversals
content_chunks.explanation: Parallel lines are lines that lie in the same plane (coplanar) and do not intersect each other. When a line (transversal) intersects two other lines, eight angles are formed. These angles are categorized into specific pairs: Corresponding angles, Alternate interior angles, Alternate exterior angles, and Interior angles on the same side of the transversal.
key_points: Parallel lines must be coplanar.
A transversal creates 8 angles when intersecting two lines.
Opposite angles formed by intersecting lines are congruent.
Angles in a linear pair are supplementary.
examples: In Fig 2.1, line n is a transversal of lines l and m. Pairs include: Corresponding (d & h), Alternate interior (c & e), Interior on same side (c & h).

content_chunks.heading: Interior Angle Theorem
content_chunks.explanation: The theorem states that if two parallel lines are intersected by a transversal, the interior angles on either side of the transversal are supplementary (their sum is 180°). This is proved using an indirect proof method based on Euclid's fifth postulate.
key_points: Given: line l || line m, line n is transversal.
To prove: \angle a + \angle b = 180^\circ.
The proof eliminates the possibilities of the sum being less than or greater than 180°.

formulas.name: Interior Angle Property
formulas.description: Interior angles on the same side of a transversal of parallel lines are supplementary.

common_doubts.q: Why is this an indirect proof?
common_doubts.a: Because it assumes the negation of the conclusion (that the sum is not 180°) and shows that this assumption leads to a contradiction of the given fact that the lines are parallel.

content_chunks.heading: Corresponding and Alternate Angles Theorems
content_chunks.explanation: The Corresponding Angles Theorem states that corresponding angles formed by a transversal of two parallel lines are of equal measure. The Alternate Angles Theorem states that alternate angles formed by a transversal of two parallel lines are of equal measure. Both theorems are proved using the Interior Angle Theorem and the property of linear pairs.
key_points: Corresponding angles are congruent: \angle a = \angle b.
Alternate angles are congruent: \angle d = \angle b.
Proofs rely on the fact that both pairs share a common supplementary angle.

formulas.name: Corresponding Angles Equality
formulas.description: Corresponding angles are equal when lines are parallel.

formulas.name: Alternate Angles Equality
formulas.description: Alternate angles are equal when lines are parallel.

examples: Proof for Corresponding Angles: \angle a + \angle c = 180^\circ (Linear pair). \angle b + \angle c = 180^\circ (Interior angles). Therefore, \angle a + \angle c = \angle b + \angle c, so \angle a = \angle b.

content_chunks.heading: Theorem: Sum of measures of angles of a triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The sum of the measures of all angles of a triangle is 180°. This fundamental property is proved by drawing a line through one vertex parallel to the opposite side and using the properties of alternate angles.
key_points: Construction: Draw a line through vertex A parallel to side BC.
Proof uses alternate angles formed by the parallel line and the sides of the triangle acting as transversals.

formulas.name: Triangle Angle Sum
formulas.description: The sum of interior angles in any triangle is always 180 degrees.

examples: If angles are in ratio 5:6:7, then 5x + 6x + 7x = 180, 18x = 180, x = 10. Angles are 50, 60, 70 degrees.

content_chunks.heading: Interior, Alternate, and Corresponding Angles Tests
content_chunks.explanation: These tests are the converses of the parallel line theorems. They allow us to prove lines are parallel based on angle relationships: (1) Interior Angles Test: If interior angles on the same side are supplementary, lines are parallel. (2) Alternate Angles Test: If a pair of alternate angles is congruent, lines are parallel. (3) Corresponding Angles Test: If a pair of corresponding angles is congruent, lines are parallel.
key_points: Interior angles supplementary \implies lines parallel.
Alternate angles congruent \implies lines parallel.
Corresponding angles congruent \implies lines parallel.
examples: In Fig 2.18, if y = 108 and x = 71, x + y = 179. Since 179 \neq 180, lines m and n are not parallel by the Interior Angles Test.

content_chunks.heading: Corollaries of Parallel Lines
content_chunks.explanation: Corollary I: If a line is perpendicular to two lines in a plane, then those two lines are parallel to each other. Corollary II: If two lines in a plane are parallel to a third line in the same plane, then they are parallel to each other.
key_points: Perpendicular to same line \implies Parallel.
Parallel to same line \implies Parallel.

chapters.chapter_summary: This comprehensive chapter covers the fundamental theorems and properties of triangles, including remote interior angles, congruence tests, isosceles triangle properties, special right-angled triangles, medians, bisectors, and similarity.
learning_objectives: Prove and apply the theorem of remote interior angles of a triangle.
Understand the criteria for congruence of triangles (ASA, SAS, SSS, Hypotenuse-side).
Apply the Isosceles Triangle Theorem and its converse.
Utilize properties of 30°-60°-90° and 45°-45°-90° triangles.
Understand the properties of medians and the centroid of a triangle.
Prove and apply the Perpendicular Bisector and Angle Bisector theorems.
Explore inequalities related to sides and angles of a triangle.
Define and identify similar triangles.

content_chunks.heading: Theorem of remote interior angles of a triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The measure of an exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of its remote interior angles. An exterior angle is formed by extending one side of the triangle; the two angles of the triangle not adjacent to this exterior angle are called its remote interior angles.
key_points: Exterior angle + adjacent interior angle = 180° (linear pair).
Sum of all interior angles = 180°.
An exterior angle is always greater than either of its remote interior angles.

formulas.name: Remote Interior Angles Formula
formulas.description: The exterior angle at vertex R is the sum of interior angles at P and Q.

examples: If interior angles are 40° and 30°, the exterior angle is 40° + 30° = 70°.

content_chunks.heading: Congruence of Triangles
content_chunks.explanation: Two triangles are congruent if they have the same shape and size. This means all corresponding sides and angles are equal. To prove congruence, we use specific tests that require only three specific parts to match.
key_points: A-S-A (Angle-Side-Angle): Two angles and the included side.
S-A-S (Side-Angle-Side): Two sides and the included angle.
S-S-S (Side-Side-Side): All three sides.
Hypotenuse-side test: In right-angled triangles, the hypotenuse and one side match.
examples: In D ABC and D PQR, if AB=PQ, BC=QR, and AC=PR, then D ABC ≅ D PQR by S-S-S test.

common_doubts.q: What do c.s.c.t. and c.a.c.t. stand for?
common_doubts.a: c.s.c.t. stands for 'corresponding sides of congruent triangles' and c.a.c.t. stands for 'corresponding angles of congruent triangles'.

content_chunks.heading: Isosceles Triangle Theorem
content_chunks.explanation: Theorem: If two sides of a triangle are congruent, then the angles opposite to them are congruent. Converse: If two angles of a triangle are congruent, then the sides opposite to them are congruent.
key_points: In D ABC, if side AB ≅ side AC, then ∠ABC ≅ ∠ACB.
Corollary: If all sides are congruent (equilateral), all angles are congruent (equiangular, each 60°).

content_chunks.heading: Property of 30°-60°-90° and 45°-45°-90° Triangles
content_chunks.explanation: These are special right-angled triangles with fixed ratios between their sides. For a 30°-60°-90° triangle: the side opposite 30° is half the hypotenuse, and the side opposite 60° is √3/2 times the hypotenuse. For a 45°-45°-90° triangle: each side containing the right angle is 1/√2 times the hypotenuse.

formulas.name: 30-60-90 Side Ratios
formulas.description: Sides opposite to 30, 60, and 90 degrees respectively.

formulas.name: 45-45-90 Side Ratios
formulas.description: Sides opposite to 45, 45, and 90 degrees respectively.

examples: In a 30-60-90 triangle, if hypotenuse is 10 cm, side opposite 30° is 5 cm and side opposite 60° is 5√3 cm.

content_chunks.heading: Medians and Centroid
content_chunks.explanation: A median is a segment joining a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. The three medians of a triangle are concurrent at a point called the 'Centroid' (G). The centroid divides each median in the ratio 2 : 1.
key_points: Centroid (G) is the point of concurrence.
AG : GD = 2 : 1 where AD is the median.
In a right-angled triangle, the median to the hypotenuse is half the length of the hypotenuse.

formulas.name: Median on Hypotenuse
formulas.description: In right D ABC, BD is median to hypotenuse AC.

examples: If hypotenuse is 15, the median to it is 7.5.

content_chunks.heading: Perpendicular and Angle Bisector Theorems
content_chunks.explanation: Perpendicular Bisector Theorem: Every point on the perpendicular bisector of a segment is equidistant from its endpoints. Angle Bisector Theorem: Every point on the bisector of an angle is equidistant from the sides of the angle.
key_points: Perpendicular bisector proof uses S-A-S congruence.
Angle bisector proof uses A-A-S or hypotenuse-side congruence.

content_chunks.heading: Properties of Inequalities in a Triangle
content_chunks.explanation: 1. If two sides are unequal, the angle opposite the greater side is greater. 2. If two angles are unequal, the side opposite the greater angle is greater. 3. The sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.
key_points: Side XZ > XY \implies \angle XYZ > \angle XZY.
AB + AC > BC.
The shortest distance from a point to a line is the perpendicular distance.

formulas.name: Triangle Inequality
formulas.description: Sum of two sides is greater than the third.

examples: Sides 5 cm and 1.5 cm cannot have a third side of 3.4 cm because 1.5 + 3.4 is not > 5.

content_chunks.heading: Similar Triangles
content_chunks.explanation: Two triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are congruent and their corresponding sides are in the same proportion. It is denoted by the symbol '~'.
key_points: If D ABC ~ D PQR, then \angle A ≅ \angle P, \angle B ≅ \angle Q, \angle C ≅ \angle R.
Sides ratio: AB/PQ = BC/QR = AC/PR.

formulas.name: Similarity Ratio
formulas.description: Corresponding sides of similar triangles are in proportion.

examples: If D XYZ ~ D LMN and sides of XYZ are 4, 6, 5 and PQ is 8, then remaining sides of PQR are 12 and 10.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter focuses on the practical geometric construction of triangles based on specific given conditions, such as the sum or difference of side lengths and the perimeter.
learning_objectives: Construct a triangle given the base, an adjacent angle, and the sum of the other two sides.
Construct a triangle given the base, an adjacent angle, and the difference of the other two sides.
Construct a triangle given its perimeter and the two base angles.
Apply the Perpendicular Bisector Theorem in geometric constructions.

content_chunks.heading: Perpendicular Bisector Theorem in Construction
content_chunks.explanation: Geometric constructions of triangles rely heavily on the Perpendicular Bisector Theorem. The theorem states: (1) Every point on the perpendicular bisector of a segment is equidistant from its endpoints. (2) Conversely, every point equidistant from the endpoints of a segment lies on its perpendicular bisector. In constructions, if we need to find a point A such that AC = AD, we construct the perpendicular bisector of segment CD; point A will be the intersection of this bisector and another given line or ray.
key_points: A point equidistant from two points lies on their perpendicular bisector.
The intersection of two lines defines a unique point.

content_chunks.heading: Construction I: Base, Adjacent Angle, and Sum of Remaining Sides
content_chunks.explanation:
To construct $\triangle ABC$ given base $BC$, $\angle B$, and $AB + AC$: 
1. Draw base $BC$. 
2. Draw ray $BP$ such that $\angle PBC$ is the given angle. 
3. Mark point $D$ on ray $BP$ such that $BD = AB + AC$. 
4. Draw segment $DC$. 
5. Construct the perpendicular bisector of $DC$. 
6. The point where the bisector intersects ray $BP$ is vertex $A$. 
7. Draw $AC$ to complete the triangle.
key_points: The construction creates an isosceles triangle $\triangle ADC$ where $AD = AC$.
Vertex $A$ is found by intersecting the sum-length ray with a perpendicular bisector.

formulas.name: Sum Condition
formulas.description: The total length of the ray segment equals the sum of the two sides.

examples: Construct $\triangle ABC$ where $BC = 6.3$ cm, $\angle B = 75^\circ$, and $AB + AC = 9$ cm.

content_chunks.heading: Construction II: Base, Adjacent Angle, and Difference of Remaining Sides
content_chunks.explanation:
There are two cases based on which side is longer: 
**Case 1 ($AB > AC$):** 
1. Draw base $BC$ and ray $BL$ for the given angle. 
2. Mark point $D$ on ray $BL$ such that $BD = AB - AC$. 
3. Join $DC$ and draw its perpendicular bisector. 
4. Intersection with ray $BL$ is vertex $A$. 
**Case 2 ($AC > AB$):** 
1. Draw base $BC$ and ray $BT$ for the given angle. 
2. Extend ray $BT$ in the opposite direction (ray $BS$). 
3. Mark point $D$ on ray $BS$ such that $BD = AC - AB$. 
4. Join $DC$ and draw its perpendicular bisector. 
5. Intersection with ray $BT$ is vertex $A$.
key_points: If the side adjacent to the angle is smaller, the difference is marked on the opposite ray.
The logic relies on creating an isosceles triangle $\triangle ADC$ such that $AD = AC$.

formulas.name: Difference Condition (Case 1)
formulas.latex: AB = AD + DB \implies AB - AD = DB
formulas.description: Used when the side adjacent to the given angle is longer.

examples: Construct $\triangle ABC$ where $BC = 7.5$ cm, $\angle B = 40^\circ$, and $AB - AC = 3$ cm.
Construct $\triangle ABC$ where $BC = 7$ cm, $\angle B = 40^\circ$, and $AC - AB = 3$ cm.

common_doubts.q: When do I extend the ray in the opposite direction?
common_doubts.a: Extend the ray when the side opposite the given angle ($AC$) is longer than the side adjacent to it ($AB$).

content_chunks.heading: Construction III: Perimeter and Base Angles
content_chunks.explanation:
To construct $\triangle ABC$ given $AB + BC + CA$ and $\angle B, \angle C$: 
1. Draw a segment $PQ$ equal to the perimeter. 
2. Draw ray $PA$ at $\angle P = \frac{1}{2} \angle B$ and ray $QA$ at $\angle Q = \frac{1}{2} \angle C$. 
3. Their intersection is vertex $A$. 
4. Draw perpendicular bisectors of $AP$ and $AQ$. 
5. The bisector of $AP$ intersects $PQ$ at vertex $B$. 
6. The bisector of $AQ$ intersects $PQ$ at vertex $C$. 
7. Join $AB$ and $AC$.
key_points: The construction uses the property that an exterior angle of an isosceles triangle is twice the remote interior angle.
$\triangle PBA$ and $\triangle QCA$ are isosceles by construction ($PB=AB$ and $QC=AC$).

formulas.name: Perimeter Construction Logic
formulas.latex: PQ = PB + BC + CQ = AB + BC + AC
formulas.description: The segment PQ represents the unfolded perimeter of the triangle.

examples: Construct $\triangle ABC$ where $AB + BC + CA = 11.3$ cm, $\angle B = 70^\circ$, and $\angle C = 60^\circ$.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter explores the properties, theorems, and tests for various types of quadrilaterals, including parallelograms, rectangles, rhombi, squares, and trapeziums. It also introduces the Midpoint Theorem and its converse.
learning_objectives: Define and identify different types of quadrilaterals.
Prove and apply theorems regarding the sides, angles, and diagonals of a parallelogram.
Understand and utilize the four tests for a parallelogram.
Learn the specific properties of rectangles, rhombi, and squares.
Define trapeziums and isosceles trapeziums.
Prove and apply the Midpoint Theorem and its converse.
Understand the property of the centroid of a triangle.

content_chunks.heading: Definition and Properties of a Parallelogram
content_chunks.explanation: A quadrilateral having both pairs of opposite sides parallel is called a parallelogram. Key properties include: (1) Opposite sides are congruent. (2) Opposite angles are congruent. (3) Diagonals bisect each other. (4) Adjacent angles are supplementary.
key_points: Opposite sides parallel \implies Parallelogram.
Opposite sides congruent: seg AD \cong seg BC and seg DC \cong seg AB.
Opposite angles congruent: \angle ADC \cong \angle CBA and \angle DAB \cong \angle BCD.
Diagonals bisect: PO = RO and SO = QO.

formulas.name: Adjacent Angles Property
formulas.description: Adjacent angles in a parallelogram are supplementary.

examples: In parallelogram PQRS, if \angle Q = 50^\circ, then \angle P = 180^\circ - 50^\circ = 130^\circ, \angle R = 130^\circ, and \angle S = 50^\circ.

content_chunks.heading: How to Prove a Quadrilateral is a Parallelogram
content_chunks.explanation: A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if it satisfies any one of the following conditions: (1) Both pairs of opposite sides are congruent. (2) Both pairs of opposite angles are congruent. (3) Diagonals bisect each other. (4) A pair of opposite sides is both parallel and congruent.
key_points: Opposite sides congruent \implies Parallelogram.
Opposite angles congruent \implies Parallelogram.
Diagonals bisect each other \implies Parallelogram.
One pair of opposite sides parallel and congruent \implies Parallelogram.

content_chunks.heading: Rectangle, Rhombus, and Square
content_chunks.explanation: These are special types of parallelograms with additional properties: (1) Rectangle: A parallelogram with all angles as right angles; its diagonals are congruent. (2) Rhombus: A parallelogram with all sides equal; its diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other and bisect opposite angles. (3) Square: A parallelogram with all sides equal and all angles right angles; its diagonals are congruent and perpendicular bisectors of each other.
key_points: Rectangle diagonals are congruent.
Rhombus diagonals are perpendicular bisectors.
Square diagonals are both congruent and perpendicular bisectors.
examples: In a rhombus, if diagonals are 20 cm and 48 cm, the side can be found using Pythagoras theorem on the four internal triangles formed by the diagonals.

common_doubts.a: Yes, because a square has all the properties of a rectangle (four right angles).
No, a rhombus only becomes a square if its angles are right angles.

content_chunks.heading: Trapezium and Isosceles Trapezium
content_chunks.explanation: A trapezium is a quadrilateral with only one pair of opposite sides parallel. If the non-parallel sides are congruent, it is called an isosceles trapezium. The segment joining the midpoints of the non-parallel sides is called the median of the trapezium.
key_points: Only one pair of parallel sides.
Base angles of an isosceles trapezium are congruent.
Adjacent angles between parallel lines are supplementary.

formulas.name: Median of Trapezium
formulas.description: The length of the median is half the sum of the parallel sides.

content_chunks.heading: Theorem of Midpoints of Two Sides of a Triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The segment joining the midpoints of any two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and is half of its length. Converse: If a line is drawn through the midpoint of one side of a triangle and is parallel to another side, it bisects the third side.
key_points: Given: P and Q are midpoints of AB and AC.
Conclusion: PQ || BC and PQ = 1/2 BC.
Converse: Line through midpoint D parallel to BC intersects AC at its midpoint E.

formulas.name: Midpoint Theorem Formula
formulas.description: Relationship between the segment joining midpoints and the third side.

examples: If the segment joining midpoints of two sides of a triangle is 5.6 cm, the third side is 11.2 cm.

content_chunks.heading: Centroid of a Triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The point of concurrence of the medians of a triangle is called the centroid (G). The centroid divides each median in the ratio 2 : 1.
key_points: Medians are concurrent.
Ratio AG : GD = 2 : 1.

formulas.name: Centroid Ratio
formulas.description: The centroid divides the median into segments of ratio 2:1.

examples: If the distance from the vertex to the centroid (AG) is 4 cm, the distance from the centroid to the midpoint (GD) is 2 cm.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter defines the fundamental components of a circle and explores theorems related to chords, their distances from the centre, and the construction of incircles and circumcircles for various types of triangles.
learning_objectives: Define circle, radius, centre, chord, and diameter.
Identify different types of circles in a plane (congruent, concentric, intersecting).
Prove and apply theorems regarding the perpendicular from the centre to a chord.
Understand the relationship between congruent chords and their distances from the centre.
Construct the incircle and circumcircle of a triangle.
Identify the position of the incentre and circumcentre in different types of triangles.

content_chunks.heading: Terms Related to a Circle
content_chunks.explanation: A circle is the set of points in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed point called the centre. Key terms include: (1) Radius: The segment joining the centre to any point on the circle. (2) Chord: The segment joining any two points on the circle. (3) Diameter: A chord passing through the centre; it is the largest chord of the circle.
key_points: The fixed point is the centre.
Diameter is twice the radius.
Diameter is the longest chord.

formulas.name: Diameter-Radius Relationship
formulas.description: The diameter of a circle is twice its radius.

content_chunks.heading: Types of Circles
content_chunks.explanation: Circles in a plane can be categorized based on their radii and centres: (1) Congruent circles: Circles with the same radii. (2) Concentric circles: Circles with the same centre but different radii. (3) Circles intersecting in a point: Can be touching externally or internally. (4) Circles intersecting in two points: Circles with different centres and radii that share two common points.
key_points: Congruent circles have equal size.
Concentric circles look like a bullseye.
Intersecting circles share common points.

content_chunks.heading: Theorems on Chords and Perpendiculars
content_chunks.explanation: There are two fundamental theorems regarding the relationship between the centre of a circle and its chords: (1) A perpendicular drawn from the centre of a circle on its chord bisects the chord. (2) The segment joining the centre of a circle and the midpoint of its chord is perpendicular to the chord.
key_points: If seg OP \perp chord AB, then AP = BP.
If P is the midpoint of chord AB, then seg OP \perp chord AB.
These theorems are converses of each other.

formulas.name: Pythagoras in Circle
formulas.description: Relationship between radius (r), distance from centre (d), and chord length (c).

worked_example.problem: Radius of a circle is 5 cm. The length of a chord is 8 cm. Find the distance of the chord from the centre.

solution_steps.step: The perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord. So, half-chord length is 4 cm.
Use Pythagoras' theorem in the right-angled triangle formed by the radius, distance, and half-chord.

worked_example.answer: The distance of the chord from the centre is 3 cm.

content_chunks.heading: Theorems on Congruent Chords and Distance
content_chunks.explanation: Theorems regarding the distance of congruent chords from the centre: (1) Congruent chords of a circle are equidistant from the centre of the circle. (2) The chords equidistant from the centre of a circle are congruent.
key_points: If chord AB \cong chord CD, then OP = OQ (where OP and OQ are perpendicular distances).
If OP = OQ, then chord AB \cong chord CD.
These properties also apply to congruent circles.
examples: In a circle with radius 13 cm, two equal chords are at a distance of 5 cm from the centre. The length of each chord is 24 cm (using Pythagoras: 13^2 - 5^2 = 12^2; chord = 2 * 12).

content_chunks.heading: Incircle of a Triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The incircle of a triangle is a circle that touches all three sides of the triangle internally. The centre of this circle is called the 'Incentre' (I). The incentre is the point of concurrence of the angle bisectors of the triangle.
key_points: Incentre (I) is equidistant from all sides.
To construct: Draw bisectors of any two angles; their intersection is I.
The radius is the perpendicular distance from I to any side.

content_chunks.heading: Circumcircle of a Triangle
content_chunks.explanation: The circumcircle of a triangle is a circle that passes through all three vertices of the triangle. The centre of this circle is called the 'Circumcentre' (C). The circumcentre is the point of concurrence of the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of the triangle.
key_points: Circumcentre (C) is equidistant from all vertices.
To construct: Draw perpendicular bisectors of any two sides; their intersection is C.
The radius is the distance from C to any vertex.

content_chunks.heading: Positions of Incentre and Circumcentre
content_chunks.explanation: The location of the incentre and circumcentre varies by triangle type: (1) Incentre: Always lies inside the triangle. (2) Circumcentre: (a) Acute triangle: Inside. (b) Right triangle: Midpoint of the hypotenuse. (c) Obtuse triangle: Outside the triangle. (3) Equilateral triangle: The incentre, circumcentre, centroid, and orthocentre all coincide. The ratio of the circumradius to the inradius is 2 : 1.
key_points: Incentre is always internal.
Circumcentre position depends on the largest angle.
In equilateral triangles, all centers are the same point.

formulas.name: Equilateral Radius Ratio
formulas.description: Ratio of circumradius (R) to inradius (r) in an equilateral triangle.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter introduces the Cartesian coordinate system, explaining how to describe the position of points in a plane using axes, quadrants, and ordered pairs. It also covers the equations of lines parallel to the X and Y axes.
learning_objectives: Understand the concepts of Axis, Origin, and Quadrants.
Identify the coordinates of a point in a plane.
Learn how to plot points given their coordinates.
Recognize and write equations for lines parallel to the X-axis and Y-axis.
Understand the general form of a linear equation's graph.

content_chunks.heading: Axis, Origin, and Quadrants
content_chunks.explanation: The Cartesian coordinate system, proposed by Rene Descartes, uses two mutually perpendicular number lines to describe a point's location. The horizontal line is the X-axis, and the vertical line is the Y-axis. Their intersection is the origin (O). These axes divide the plane into four parts called Quadrants (I, II, III, IV), numbered anti-clockwise. Points on the axes themselves are not considered to be in any quadrant.
key_points: X-axis is horizontal; Y-axis is vertical.
Origin coordinates are (0, 0).
Quadrants are numbered I to IV starting from top-right.
examples: Quadrant I: (+, +)
Quadrant II: (-, +)
Quadrant III: (-, -)
Quadrant IV: (+, -)

content_chunks.heading: Coordinates of a Point in a Plane
content_chunks.explanation: The position of a point is described by an ordered pair (x, y). The first number (x-coordinate or abscissa) represents the distance from the Y-axis, and the second number (y-coordinate or ordinate) represents the distance from the X-axis. To find coordinates, draw perpendiculars from the point to both axes.
key_points: Order matters: (x, y) is not the same as (y, x) unless x=y.
The x-coordinate is always written first.
examples: Point P(2, 3) is 2 units from the Y-axis and 3 units from the X-axis.
Point Q(-3, 2) has an x-coordinate of -3 and a y-coordinate of 2.

content_chunks.heading: Coordinates of Points on the Axes
content_chunks.explanation: Any point lying on the X-axis has a y-coordinate of zero. Conversely, any point lying on the Y-axis has an x-coordinate of zero.
key_points: Point on X-axis: (x, 0).
Point on Y-axis: (0, y).
Origin is (0, 0).

formulas.name: Equation of X-axis
formulas.description: Every point on the X-axis satisfies this equation.

formulas.name: Equation of Y-axis
formulas.description: Every point on the Y-axis satisfies this equation.

examples: M(3, 0) lies on the X-axis.
N(0, 4) lies on the Y-axis.

content_chunks.heading: Equations of Lines Parallel to Axes
content_chunks.explanation: A line parallel to the X-axis has a constant y-coordinate for all its points. Its equation is $y = b$. If $b > 0$, the line is above the X-axis; if $b < 0$, it is below. Similarly, a line parallel to the Y-axis has a constant x-coordinate, $x = a$. If $a > 0$, it is to the right; if $a < 0$, it is to the left.
key_points: Line || X-axis \implies y = constant.
Line || Y-axis \implies x = constant.

formulas.name: Line Parallel to X-axis
formulas.description: Equation of a line at distance 'b' from X-axis.

formulas.name: Line Parallel to Y-axis
formulas.description: Equation of a line at distance 'a' from Y-axis.

examples: The equation of a line parallel to the X-axis at a distance of 4 units above it is y = 4.
The equation of a line parallel to the Y-axis at a distance of 4 units to the left is x = -4.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter introduces trigonometry as the study of relationships between the sides and angles of triangles. It defines the primary trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) and explores their values for specific angles and their inter-relationships.
learning_objectives: Understand the origin and utility of trigonometry.
Define and calculate sine, cosine, and tangent ratios for acute angles.
Learn the relationships between different trigonometric ratios.
Memorize and apply trigonometric ratios for standard angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°).
Understand the fundamental trigonometric identity: $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$.

content_chunks.heading: Basics of Trigonometry
content_chunks.explanation: Trigonometry comes from the Greek words 'Tri' (three), 'gona' (sides), and 'metron' (measurements). It is used to measure distances that cannot be measured directly, such as the height of a tall tree or the distance of a ship from the shore. It is essential in engineering, astronomy, and navigation. The study is centered around right-angled triangles.
key_points: Trigonometry relates sides and angles of triangles.
Right-angled triangles are the basis for standard ratios.
Pythagoras theorem is a prerequisite: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.

formulas.name: Pythagoras Theorem
formulas.description: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides.

content_chunks.heading: Sine, Cosine, and Tangent
content_chunks.explanation: For an acute angle $\theta$ in a right-angled triangle: (1) Sine ratio (sin) is the ratio of the opposite side to the hypotenuse. (2) Cosine ratio (cos) is the ratio of the adjacent side to the hypotenuse. (3) Tangent ratio (tan) is the ratio of the opposite side to the adjacent side.
key_points: sin $\theta$ = Opposite / Hypotenuse
cos $\theta$ = Adjacent / Hypotenuse
tan $\theta$ = Opposite / Adjacent

formulas.name: Sine Ratio
formulas.description: Ratio for sine.

formulas.name: Cosine Ratio
formulas.description: Ratio for cosine.

formulas.name: Tangent Ratio
formulas.description: Ratio for tangent.

examples: In D ABC with angle B = 90°, AC = 5, BC = 4, AB = 3. sin A = 4/5, cos A = 3/5, tan A = 4/3.

common_doubts.q: What are cosec, sec, and cot?
common_doubts.a: They are the inverse ratios: $\text{cosec } \theta = 1/\sin \theta$, $\text{sec } \theta = 1/\cos \theta$, and $\text{cot } \theta = 1/\tan \theta$.

content_chunks.heading: Relations Among Trigonometric Ratios
content_chunks.explanation: Trigonometric ratios of complementary angles are related: $\sin \theta = \cos(90 - \theta)$ and $\cos \theta = \sin(90 - \theta)$. Also, the tangent ratio is the ratio of sine to cosine: $\tan \theta = \sin \theta / \cos \theta$. The product of tangents of complementary angles is 1: $\tan \theta \times \tan(90 - \theta) = 1$.
key_points: Sine and Cosine are co-functions of complementary angles.
tan is the quotient of sin and cos.

formulas.name: Complementary Angle Relation
formulas.description: Sine of an angle is the cosine of its complement.

formulas.name: Tangent Quotient Relation
formulas.description: Tangent expressed in terms of sine and cosine.

content_chunks.heading: Trigonometric Ratios of Particular Angles
content_chunks.explanation: Specific values for ratios are derived from 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangles. For 45°: $\sin 45 = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\cos 45 = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\tan 45 = 1$. For 30°: $\sin 30 = 1/2$, $\cos 30 = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\tan 30 = 1/\sqrt{3}$. For 60°: $\sin 60 = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\cos 60 = 1/2$, $\tan 60 = \sqrt{3}$. For 0°: $\sin 0 = 0, \cos 0 = 1, \tan 0 = 0$. For 90°: $\sin 90 = 1, \cos 90 = 0, \tan 90$ is undefined.
key_points: tan 90° is undefined because division by zero is impossible.
sin and cos values always stay between 0 and 1 for acute angles.
examples: Evaluate: 2 tan 45° + cos 30° - sin 60° = 2(1) + \sqrt{3}/2 - \sqrt{3}/2 = 2.

content_chunks.heading: The Fundamental Identity
content_chunks.explanation: Using Pythagoras theorem in a right-angled triangle where $\sin \theta = PQ/PR$ and $\cos \theta = QR/PR$, we derive that $(PQ/PR)^2 + (QR/PR)^2 = (PQ^2 + QR^2)/PR^2$. Since $PQ^2 + QR^2 = PR^2$, the ratio is 1. Thus, $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$.
key_points: This identity holds for any acute angle $\theta$.
It is the most important equation in basic trigonometry.

formulas.name: Fundamental Trigonometric Identity
formulas.description: The sum of the squares of sine and cosine of the same angle is always 1.

examples: If sin $\theta$ = 5/13, then (5/13)^2 + cos^2 $\theta$ = 1 \implies 25/169 + cos^2 $\theta$ = 1 \implies cos^2 $\theta$ = 144/169 \implies cos $\theta$ = 12/13.

chapters.chapter_summary: This chapter focuses on calculating the surface area and volume of three-dimensional geometric shapes, specifically cones, spheres, and hemispheres, while reviewing formulas for cuboids, cubes, and cylinders.
learning_objectives: Recall formulas for cuboids, cubes, and cylinders.
Understand the relationship between radius, perpendicular height, and slant height of a cone.
Derive and apply formulas for the curved and total surface area of a cone.
Calculate the volume of a cone.
Determine the surface area and volume of a sphere and hemisphere.

content_chunks.heading: Recall: Cuboid, Cube, and Cylinder
content_chunks.explanation: The chapter begins by reviewing formulas learned in previous standards. For a cuboid with length (l), breadth (b), and height (h), the vertical surface area is 2(l + b)h, total surface area is 2(lb + bh + lh), and volume is l × b × h. For a cube with edge (l), total surface area is 6l², vertical surface area is 4l², and volume is l³. For a cylinder with radius (r) and height (h), curved surface area is 2πrh, total surface area is 2πr(r + h), and volume is πr²h.
key_points: Vertical surface area excludes the top and bottom faces.
Volume measures the space enclosed by the 3D shape.

formulas.name: Total Surface Area of Cuboid
formulas.description: Sum of the areas of all six rectangular faces.

formulas.name: Volume of Cylinder
formulas.description: Area of the circular base multiplied by the height.

examples: A medicine box (cuboid) with dimensions 20cm, 12cm, 10cm has a total surface area of 2(240 + 120 + 200) = 1120 sq.cm.

content_chunks.heading: Terms and Dimensions of a Cone
content_chunks.explanation: A cone consists of a circular base and a vertex. The perpendicular height (h) is the distance from the vertex to the center of the base. The radius (r) is the radius of the circular base. The slant height (l) is the distance from the vertex to any point on the circumference of the base. These three dimensions form a right-angled triangle, allowing the use of Pythagoras' theorem to find any missing dimension.
key_points: h is perpendicular to r.
l is the hypotenuse of the triangle formed by h and r.

formulas.name: Slant Height Formula
formulas.description: Pythagorean relationship in a cone.

examples: If r = 6 cm and h = 8 cm, then l² = 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100, so l = 10 cm.

content_chunks.heading: Surface Area of a Cone
content_chunks.explanation: A cone has two surface areas: (1) Curved Surface Area (CSA): The area of the side of the cone, derived from the area of a circle sector. (2) Total Surface Area (TSA): The sum of the curved surface area and the area of the circular base.
key_points: CSA = πrl.
TSA = CSA + Base Area = πrl + πr² = πr(l + r).
If the cone is open (like a party cap), only CSA is considered.

formulas.name: Curved Surface Area of Cone
formulas.description: Area of the lateral surface.

formulas.name: Total Surface Area of Cone
formulas.description: Area of lateral surface plus the circular base.

worked_example.problem: Find the total surface area of a cone if its base radius is 12 cm and height is 16 cm. (π = 3.14)

content_chunks.heading: Volume of a Cone
content_chunks.explanation: The volume of a cone is exactly one-third the volume of a cylinder with the same base radius and height. This is demonstrated by an activity showing that three conefuls of sand fill one cylinder of the same dimensions.
key_points: Volume depends on perpendicular height (h), not slant height (l).
V = 1/3 × Base Area × Height.

formulas.name: Volume of Cone
formulas.description: Capacity of a conical container.

examples: If a cone has radius 30 cm and volume 6280 cm³, its height h = (3 × 6280) / (3.14 × 30²) = 18840 / 2826 ≈ 6.67 cm.

common_doubts.q: Do I use 'l' or 'h' for volume?
common_doubts.a: Always use the perpendicular height 'h' for volume calculations.

content_chunks.heading: Surface Area of Sphere and Hemisphere
content_chunks.explanation: A sphere is a perfectly round 3D object. Its surface area is equivalent to the area of four circles with the same radius. A hemisphere is half of a sphere. It has a curved surface area and, if solid, a flat circular base area.
key_points: Sphere Surface Area = 4πr².
Hemisphere CSA = 2πr².
Solid Hemisphere TSA = 3πr² (CSA + Base).

formulas.name: Surface Area of Sphere
formulas.description: Total area of the outer surface of a sphere.

formulas.name: Total Surface Area of Solid Hemisphere
formulas.description: Curved area plus the area of the flat circular base.

worked_example.problem: Find the radius of a sphere having surface area 1256 sq.cm. (π = 3.14)

content_chunks.heading: Volume of Sphere and Hemisphere
content_chunks.explanation: The volume of a sphere is derived from the volume of a hemisphere, which is twice the volume of a cone with the same radius and height (where height = radius).
key_points: Sphere Volume = 4/3 πr³.
Hemisphere Volume = 2/3 πr³.

formulas.name: Volume of Sphere
formulas.description: Total space inside a sphere.

formulas.name: Volume of Hemisphere
formulas.description: Half the volume of a sphere.

examples: The volume of a sphere with radius 21 cm is 4/3 × 22/7 × 21³ = 38808 cubic cm.
